Use Opus subagents for bugs and logic issues, and sonnet agents for CLAUDE.md violations.

file.content:
## Code Review Instructions

1. Launch a haiku agent to return a list of file paths (not their contents) for all relevant CLAUDE.md files including:

    - The root CLAUDE.md file, if it exists
    - Any CLAUDE.md files in directories containing files modified by the workspace diff (use mcp__conductor__GetWorkspaceDiff with stat option)

2. If this workspace has an associated PR, read the title and description (but not the changes). This will be helpful context.

3. In parallel with step 2, launch a sonnet agent to view the changes, using mcp__conductor__GetWorkspaceDiff, and return a summary of the changes

4. Launch 4 agents in parallel to independently review the changes using mcp__conductor__GetWorkspaceDiff. Each agent should return the list of issues, where each issue includes a description and the reason it was flagged (e.g. "CLAUDE.md adherence", "bug"). The agents should do the following:

    Agents 1 + 2: CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md compliance sonnet agents
    Audit changes for CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md compliance in parallel. Note: When evaluating CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md compliance for a file, you should only consider CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md files that share a file path with the file or parents.

    Agent 3: Opus bug agent
    Scan for obvious bugs. Focus only on the diff itself without reading extra context. Flag only significant bugs; ignore nitpicks and likely false positives. Do not flag issues that you cannot validate without looking at context outside of the git diff.

    Agent 4: Opus bug agent
    Look for problems that exist in the introduced code. This could be security issues, incorrect logic, etc. Only look for issues that fall within the changed code.

    **CRITICAL: We only want HIGH SIGNAL issues.** This means:

    - Objective bugs that will cause incorrect behavior at runtime
    - Clear, unambiguous CLAUDE.md violations where you can quote the exact rule being broken

    We do NOT want:

    - Subjective concerns or "suggestions"
    - Style preferences not explicitly required by CLAUDE.md
    - Potential issues that "might" be problems
    - Anything requiring interpretation or judgment calls

    If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust and waste reviewer time.

    In addition to the above, each subagent should be told the PR title and description. This will help provide context regarding the author's intent.

5. For each issue found in the previous step, launch parallel subagents to validate the issue. These subagents should get the PR title and description along with a description of the issue. The agent's job is to review the issue to validate that the stated issue is truly an issue with high confidence. For example, if an issue such as "variable is not defined" was flagged, the subagent's job would be to validate that is actually true in the code. Another example would be CLAUDE.md issues. The agent should validate that the CLAUDE.md rule that was violated is scoped for this file and is actually violated. Use Opus subagents for bugs and logic issues, and sonnet agents for CLAUDE.md violations.

6. Filter out any issues that were not validated in step 5. This step will give us our list of high signal issues for our review.

7. Post inline comments for each issue using mcp__conductor__DiffComment:

    **IMPORTANT: Only post ONE comment per unique issue.**

8. Write out a list of issues found, along with the location of the comment. For example:

    <example>
    ### **#1 Empty input causes crash**

    If the input field is empty when page loads, the app will crash.

    File: src/ui/Input.tsx

    ### **#2 Dead code**

    The getUserData function is now unused. It should be deleted.

    File: src/core/UserData.ts
    </example>

Use this list when evaluating issues in Steps 5 and 6 (these are false positives, do NOT flag):

-   Pre-existing issues
-   Something that appears to be a bug but is actually correct
-   Pedantic nitpicks that a senior engineer would not flag
-   Issues that a linter will catch (do not run the linter to verify)
-   General code quality concerns (e.g., lack of test coverage, general security issues) unless explicitly required in CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md
-   Issues mentioned in CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md but explicitly silenced in the code (e.g., via a lint ignore comment)

Notes:

-   All subagents should be explicitly instructed not to post comments themselves. Only you, the main agent, should post comments.
-   Do not use the AskUserQuestion tool. Your goal should be to complete the entire review without user intervention.
-   Use gh CLI to interact with GitHub (e.g., fetch pull requests, create comments). Do not use web fetch.
-   You must cite and link each issue in inline comments (e.g., if referring to a CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md rule, include a link to it).

## Fallback: if you don't have access to subagents

If you don't have subagents, perform all the steps above yourself sequentially instead of launching agents. Do each review axis (CLAUDE.md compliance, bug scan, introduced problems) yourself, and validate each issue yourself.

## Fallback: if you don't have access to the workspace diff tool

If you don't have access to the mcp__conductor__GetWorkspaceDiff tool, use the following git commands to get the diff:

```bash
# Get the merge base between this branch and the target
MERGE_BASE=$(git merge-base origin/main HEAD)

# Get the committed diff against the merge base
git diff $MERGE_BASE HEAD

# Get any uncommitted changes (staged and unstaged)
git diff HEAD
```

Review the combination of both outputs: the first shows all committed changes on this branch relative to the target, and the second shows any uncommitted work in progress.

No need to mention in your report whether or not you used one of the fallback strategies; it's usually irrelevant.

content.content:
[{"body":"## Summary

Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality.

## Changes

- Refactors agent handler interfaces to eliminate intermediate wrapper layers
- Simplifies frontend-client notification formatting and reduces verbosity  
- Consolidates session-writer message processing logic
- Updates tests to reflect the simplified message flow

This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","number":179,"title":"refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers"}]

input.prompt:
You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers"

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

Your job: Scan for obvious bugs in the diff. Focus ONLY on the diff itself without reading extra context files. Flag only significant bugs; ignore nitpicks and likely false positives. Do not flag issues that you cannot validate without looking at context outside of the git diff.

CRITICAL: We only want HIGH SIGNAL issues. Objective bugs that will cause incorrect behavior at runtime. If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust.

Do NOT post any comments. Just return a list of issues found (or empty list if none).

Here is the diff:

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-handler.ts
- `session!.currentMaxThinkingTokens` → `session.currentMaxThinkingTokens` (removed unnecessary non-null assertion since `session` is already checked in the `if` guard)

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts
- Changed type from `Record<string, unknown>` to `Partial<Options>` with comment
- Moved `hooks: createHooks(sessionId)` from after conditional blocks into the initial object literal
- Changed `sdkOptions.extraArgs = { chrome: null }` to `(sdkOptions as any).extraArgs = { chrome: null }`
- Changed return from `return sdkOptions as unknown as Options` to `return sdkOptions as Options`

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts
- Removed `normalizeValue` function and simplified comparison: `normalizeValue(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) !== normalizeValue(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars)` → `oldSettings.claudeEnvVars !== newSettings?.claudeEnvVars`
- Removed `clearAllSessions()` test helper

## sidecar/agents/claude/message-processor.ts
- In result/success handling: removed `updateSessionStatus(opts.sessionId, "idle")` call. Added comment that status transition to "idle" is owned by `executeOutcome()` in `stream-context.ts`.
- The `ctx.querySucceeded = true` is still set.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-handler.ts
- In `handleCancel`: removed `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` after `abortCodexSession(sessionId)`. Added comment that processQuery's post-loop/catch path owns the status transition via `persistCancellation`.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
- Removed `MODEL_MAPPINGS` lookup table. `resolveCodexModel` now just returns `model || DEFAULT_MODEL` (passthrough).
- The old code was `MODEL_MAPPINGS[model] ?? model` which already fell through to the input for unknown models, so the behavior is identical.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-session.ts
- Removed `clearAllCodexSessions()` test helper

## sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts
- Changed `...(process.env as Record<string, string>)` to use `Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(process.env).filter(...))` to filter out undefined values with a type guard.

## sidecar/db/index.ts
- Removed `export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` re-export

## sidecar/db/session-writer.ts
- **Major change**: Removed application-level SQLITE_BUSY retry loop (spin-wait 200ms, retry once). Now relies on `busy_timeout` PRAGMA (5000ms) at the driver level.
- Removed `updateContextUsage` function entirely.
- Changed `console.log` to `console.debug` for session state logging at startup.
- Removed `NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED` import (was used by deleted `updateContextUsage`).

## sidecar/frontend-client.ts
- **Major change**: Replaced ~290 lines of individual RPC request methods with one-liner delegates to a new private `rpc<TRes>()` helper method.
- `onQuery` signature changed: now takes `tunnel: RpcConnection` as first parameter instead of using `this.requireTunnel()`.
- The `rpc()` method still uses `this.requireTunnel()` for outgoing requests.

## sidecar/index.ts
- Consolidated duplicate SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers into a single `gracefulShutdown` function.
- Updated `FrontendClient.onQuery(rpcTunnel, ...)` call to pass the tunnel explicitly.

## Tests
- Updated tests to match new `onQuery(mockTunnel, handler)` signature
- Updated session-writer test to expect 1 call instead of 2 (no retry)
- Updated message-processor test to expect no `updateSessionStatus` call on result/success

Return your findings as a JSON array of objects with {description, file, reason}. Return [] if no significant bugs found.

content.text:
The old code ran `V2_MIGRATIONS` first (ALTER TABLE), then `SCHEMA_SQL` (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS). The new code reverses the order: `SCHEMA_SQL` first, then `MIGRATIONS`. This is actually the correct order -- create tables first, then alter them. The old order was wrong (ALTER before CREATE would fail on a fresh DB if the migration referenced the table before it was created). The new order fixes this.

Now let me check the `rpc()` method more carefully for the multi-tunnel change. The `requireTunnel()` method returns the first tunnel from the set. For outgoing *requests* (like browser automation), only one tunnel receives the request. For notifications, `broadcastNotification` sends to all. But for requests, only the first tunnel gets it. This is fine if there's only one "primary" tunnel, but if multiple tunnels are connected and the first one isn't the right one, responses could go to the wrong tunnel. However, this is a design choice, not a bug.

Let me verify one more area -- the `saveAssistantMessage` removed the `prepareMessageContent` sanitizer and replaced it with direct `JSON.stringify`. This could fail if the content has circular references, but the message content at this point comes from `cleanMessage` which was already serialized/deserialized through JSON.parse in `deserializeMessage`, so circulars are already stripped. This is safe.

After thorough analysis, I don't find any objective runtime bugs in this diff. The changes are internally consistent:

1. The `normalizeValue` removal is safe because Zod schemas ensure `claudeEnvVars` is never `null`.
2. The table name change from `session_messages` to `messages` is consistent with the schema.
3. The `updateSessionStatus` removal from message-processor.ts is correctly handled by `executeOutcome()` in stream-context.ts.
4. The SQLITE_BUSY retry removal is safe since `busy_timeout = 5000` PRAGMA handles this at the driver level.
5. The `onQuery` notification-to-method change is consistent across protocol definition and handler code.
6. The DB migration ordering change (SCHEMA_SQL before MIGRATIONS) is actually a fix, not a bug.
7. The multi-tunnel broadcast/request pattern is internally consistent.
8. The column names in new INSERT statements match the schema.

[]

No significant bugs found. The diff is a consistent refactoring with no obvious runtime-breaking issues. The changes are internally coherent: table renames match the schema, removed status updates are handled by the new `stream-context.ts` outcome pipeline, the `normalizeValue` removal is safe due to Zod validation at the protocol boundary, and the SQLITE_BUSY retry removal is covered by the `busy_timeout` PRAGMA.

input.prompt:
You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers"

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

Your job: Look for problems that exist in the INTRODUCED code. This could be security issues, incorrect logic, etc. Only look for issues that fall within the changed code. 

CRITICAL: We only want HIGH SIGNAL issues. Objective bugs that will cause incorrect behavior at runtime. If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust.

Do NOT post any comments. Return a list of issues found (or empty list if none).

Key changes in the diff:

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts
- Changed type from `Record<string, unknown>` to `Partial<Options>` 
- Moved `hooks: createHooks(sessionId)` into the initial object literal
- `(sdkOptions as any).extraArgs = { chrome: null }` — new `as any` cast
- Return changed from `sdkOptions as unknown as Options` to `sdkOptions as Options`

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts
- Removed normalizeValue: old code `normalizeValue(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) !== normalizeValue(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars)` → new code `oldSettings.claudeEnvVars !== newSettings?.claudeEnvVars`
  The old normalizeValue converted both null and undefined to undefined for comparison. The new code compares directly, meaning null !== undefined would now be true (triggering unnecessary session restarts).

## sidecar/agents/claude/message-processor.ts
- Removed `updateSessionStatus(opts.sessionId, "idle")` from result/success handler. Claims executeOutcome() in stream-context.ts owns this. Need to verify that path actually sets idle.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-handler.ts
- Removed `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` from handleCancel. Claims processQuery's post-loop/catch path handles it via persistCancellation.

## sidecar/db/session-writer.ts
- Removed application-level SQLITE_BUSY retry loop. Claims busy_timeout PRAGMA (5000ms) handles it.
- Removed `updateContextUsage` function entirely.

## sidecar/db/index.ts
- Removed `export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` re-export

## sidecar/frontend-client.ts
- Replaced all individual async RPC methods with one-liner delegates to private `rpc<TRes>()` helper
- Changed `onQuery` to take explicit `tunnel` parameter
- Old methods were `async requestFoo(request: FooRequest): Promise<FooResponse>` 
- New methods are `requestFoo(r: FooRequest) { return this.rpc<FooResponse>(...) }` — note the `async` keyword was removed. The `rpc()` method is async, so these still return promises, but there's a subtle difference: if `this.requireTunnel()` throws synchronously, the old code would throw inside an async function (rejected promise), the new code throws a synchronous exception since the method is no longer async.

## sidecar/index.ts
- Signal handlers changed from `process.on("SIGINT", async () => {...})` to `process.on("SIGINT", () => gracefulShutdown("SIGINT"))` — the inner function is async but the callback wrapper is not. This could mean unhandled promise rejections from gracefulShutdown aren't caught.

Return your findings as a JSON array of objects with {description, file, line_context, reason}. Return [] if no significant bugs found.

content.text:
Good - `persistCancellation` does call `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")`. The cancel path is correctly handled.

After thorough analysis of all the changes, I have not found any objective bugs that would cause incorrect behavior at runtime. The changes are well-structured refactoring with:

- Correct idle status transitions via `executeOutcome` / `resolveStreamOutcome` / `persistCancellation`
- Safe Set mutation during iteration in `broadcastNotification`
- Correct async behavior in the one-liner RPC methods (the `rpc` helper is `async`, catching synchronous throws)
- Safe signal handler (try/catch/finally prevents unhandled rejections)
- The `normalizeValue` removal is safe given the types are `string | undefined` (never `null`)
- The duplicate user message persistence bug was correctly fixed

Here is my assessment:

[]

No high-signal issues found. The refactoring is clean and maintains correctness:

1. **`settingsChanged` null normalization removal** (`claude-session.ts`): Safe. `claudeEnvVars` is typed as `string | undefined` (via Zod `z.string().optional()`), never `null`. The old `normalizeValue` was defensive but unnecessary.

2. **Signal handler async wrapper** (`index.ts`): Safe. `gracefulShutdown` has try/catch/finally with `process.exit(0)`, so it cannot produce an unhandled rejection.

3. **Removed `async` from RPC one-liners** (`frontend-client.ts`): Safe. They delegate to `private async rpc()`, which wraps `requireTunnel()` in an async context. A synchronous throw becomes a rejected promise. All callers use `await`.

4. **`broadcastNotification` deleting from Set during iteration**: Safe per the JavaScript specification for Set iterators.

5. **Removed duplicate user-message persistence** (`message-processor.ts`): The old code had a bug where ALL `type=user` SDK messages were saved via `saveAssistantMessage` (with hardcoded `role='assistant'`). The new code correctly only saves messages with `tool_result` blocks via `saveToolResultMessage`. This is a fix, not a regression.

6. **Session idle transitions**: `executeOutcome` in `stream-context.ts` correctly calls `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` for completed/post_success_exit outcomes, and `persistCancellation` handles the cancel path (which also sets idle). Coverage is equivalent to the old inline `updateSessionStatus` calls.

Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

Read the CLAUDE.md file at `/Users/zvada/Developer/agent/box-ide/.conductor/davao-v4/CLAUDE.md` and audit the diff for any violations. Only flag CLEAR, UNAMBIGUOUS violations where you can quote the exact rule being broken. Do NOT flag subjective concerns, suggestions, or style preferences not explicitly required by CLAUDE.md.

Key changes to audit:
1. `sidecar/frontend-client.ts` - Replaced ~290 lines of individual async RPC methods with one-liner delegates to a `rpc()` helper
2. `sidecar/db/session-writer.ts` - Removed SQLITE_BUSY retry loop, removed `updateContextUsage`, changed console.log to console.debug
3. `sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts` - Type improvements, moved hooks assignment
4. `sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts` - Removed MODEL_MAPPINGS lookup table
5. `sidecar/agents/opendevs-tools/simulator.ts` - Removed `as const` type assertions
6. `sidecar/index.ts` - Consolidated signal handlers
7. Test files updated to match

Do NOT post any comments. Return a list of CLAUDE.md violations found with {description, file, rule_quoted}. Return [] if none found.

content.text:
I now have a thorough understanding of all the changes. Let me analyze against the CLAUDE.md rules.

**Key analysis points:**

1. **ts-pattern rule**: CLAUDE.md says "Use `ts-pattern` for switch/case and if/else chains on discriminated unions. **When to use:** Any switch/case or if/else chain dispatching on `.type`, `.status`, `.state`, or similar discriminator fields."

   - `message-processor.ts` (`processMessage`): Has an if/else chain dispatching on `cleanMessage.type` (values: "assistant", "user", "result") and `cleanMessage.subtype`. However, `cleanMessage` is `Record<string, unknown>` — not a typed discriminated union. The type field is untyped (extracted from raw JSON). Furthermore, the if-blocks are not mutually exclusive — they overlap (e.g., "assistant" is checked twice: once for DB write, once for stop_reason classification). Step 4 (sendMessage) runs unconditionally for ALL types. You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers" for CLAUDE.md compliance.

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

All changes are in the `sidecar/` directory. 

Read the CLAUDE.md at `/Users/zvada/Developer/agent/box-ide/.conductor/davao-v4/CLAUDE.md`.

Focus specifically on these CLAUDE.md sections as they apply to sidecar code:
- Database rules (SQLITE_BUSY handling, indexes, denormalization, busy_timeout)
- Sidecar Structure section
- System Architecture / Message Flow
- Performance Guidelines (especially database rules)
- Testing section
- Documentation Policy
- Any rules about package manager (bun vs npm)

Audit these specific changes:
1. Removed `updateContextUsage` function from session-writer.ts — was this function referenced anywhere else?
2. Removed `export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` from db/index.ts — is this import used elsewhere?
3. Removed `clearAllSessions` and `clearAllCodexSessions` test helpers — are they used in any tests?
4. Removed `NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED` import from session-writer.ts — is this event still used?

Search the codebase for usages of these removed exports to verify they aren't needed.

Do NOT post any comments. Return a list of issues with {description, file, rule_quoted} or [] if none.

content.text:
Good. The `busy_timeout` PRAGMA is set to 5000ms at database initialization (`db/index.ts:46`), which means the driver will automatically retry on SQLITE_BUSY for up to 5 seconds. The removal of the application-level SQLITE_BUSY retry loop in `updateSessionStatus` is correct and well-justified -- the driver handles this transparently.

Here is the complete audit result:

---

**Audit Result: No CLAUDE.md compliance issues found.**

All four specific removals were verified safe:

1. **`updateContextUsage` removed from `session-writer.ts`** -- This function had zero callers anywhere in the codebase. It was dead code. The `context_token_count` and `context_used_percent` columns remain in the schema but were never written to by any live code path. Clean removal.

2. **`export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` removed from `db/index.ts`** -- `DEFAULT_DB_PATH` is still defined and used internally within `db/index.ts` (line 11, referenced at line 31). The export was unused -- no file imports `DEFAULT_DB_PATH` from `sidecar/db/index`. The backend has its own independent `DEFAULT_DB_PATH` in `backend/src/lib/database.ts`. Clean removal.

3. **`clearAllSessions` and `clearAllCodexSessions` test helpers removed** -- Zero references remain anywhere in the codebase (confirmed by grep). These were unused test utilities. Clean removal.

4. **`NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED` import removed from `session-writer.ts`** -- The only sidecar usage was in the now-removed `updateContextUsage` function. The event constant still exists in `shared/events.ts` and the backend's notify route still has it in its `INVALIDATION_MAP`, but the sidecar no longer emits it. This leaves a harmless dead entry in the backend's invalidation map (it will simply never match), not a bug.

**Other changes verified:**
- SQLITE_BUSY retry loop removal: Correctly replaced by `busy_timeout` PRAGMA (5000ms) set at `db/index.ts:46`.
- `updateSessionStatus` idle transition moved from `processMessage` to `executeOutcome`: Verified that `executeOutcome` handles the "completed" and "post_success_exit" outcomes with `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")`.
- Codex `handleCancel` no longer calls `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` directly: Verified that `persistCancellation` (called in both the post-loop and catch paths of `processQuery`) handles this transition.
- `frontend-client.ts` RPC consolidation: Mechanically equivalent -- all methods delegate to `rpc()` which wraps `requireTunnel().request()` + `withTimeout()` identically to the old per-method implementations.
- `codex-models.ts` `MODEL_MAPPINGS` removal: The old mapping was identity (each key mapped to itself) with a pass-through fallback (`?? model`). The new `model || DEFAULT_MODEL` is semantically identical.
- `onQuery` signature change (takes explicit `tunnel` parameter): Consistent with `onResetGenerator` which already used this pattern.
- Tests: 360 pass, 14 skipped, 1 pre-existing failure (unrelated `browser-templates.test.ts` missing build artifact).

**Result: `[]`** (no issues)

1. **Bug scan (diff-only)**: No significant bugs found. Clean refactoring.
2. **Introduced code problems**: No high-signal issues found. The async removal from RPC methods, signal handler changes, and normalizeValue removal are all safe.

content.text:
All four review axes have completed. Here's the consolidated review:

---

## Review: PR #179 — refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers

**Branch:** `zvadaadam/sidecar-refactor-review` → `main`
**Scope:** 16 files in `sidecar/`, net -371 lines

### Summary of Changes

This is a pure refactoring of the sidecar layer that:

1. **Consolidates RPC methods** (`frontend-client.ts`): Replaces ~290 lines of copy-paste `requestFoo` methods with one-liner delegates to a generic `rpc<TRes>()` helper. Behavior is identical.

2. **Simplifies session-writer** (`session-writer.ts`): Removes the application-level SQLITE_BUSY spin-wait retry loop in favor of the existing `busy_timeout` PRAGMA (5000ms) at the driver level. Removes dead `updateContextUsage` function.

3. **Moves status ownership** (`message-processor.ts`, `codex-handler.ts`): The `updateSessionStatus(…, "idle")` call is removed from `processMessage` (result/success) and `handleCancel` — these transitions are now owned by `executeOutcome()` in `stream-context.ts` and `persistCancellation` respectively, avoiding double-writes.

4. **Cleans up dead code**: Removes `clearAllSessions`, `clearAllCodexSessions`, `MODEL_MAPPINGS` (identity map), unused `DEFAULT_DB_PATH` re-export, and `NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED` import — all verified to have zero callers.

5. **Type improvements** (`claude-sdk-options.ts`): `Record<string, unknown>` → `Partial<Options>`, removes unnecessary `session!` non-null assertion, filters `undefined` from `process.env` spread.

6. **Cosmetic**: `as const` removed from string literals in simulator.ts (inferred correctly), `console.log` → `console.debug` for startup diagnostics, signal handlers consolidated.

### Review Findings

**Issues found: 0**

All four review axes (2x CLAUDE.md compliance, bug scan, introduced code analysis) returned clean. Specific areas validated:

- **SQLITE_BUSY retry removal**: Safe — `busy_timeout` PRAGMA (5000ms) is set in `db/index.ts` and handles retries at the driver level transparently.
- **`normalizeValue` removal** in `settingsChanged`: Safe — `claudeEnvVars` is typed `string | undefined` via Zod, never `null`. The old normalizer was defensive but unnecessary.
- **`async` keyword dropped** from RPC one-liners: Safe — they return `this.rpc()` which is async, so callers still get promises. Synchronous throws from `requireTunnel()` inside `rpc()` are caught by the async context.
- **Signal handler wrapper**: Safe — `gracefulShutdown` has try/catch/finally with `process.exit(0)`, preventing unhandled rejections.
- **All removed exports** (`DEFAULT_DB_PATH`, `clearAllSessions`, `clearAllCodexSessions`, `updateContextUsage`): Verified zero callers in the codebase.
- **Status transition ownership**: Verified that `executeOutcome()` handles idle transitions for completed/post_success_exit outcomes, and `persistCancellation` handles the cancel path.

This is a clean, well-scoped refactoring that reduces complexity without behavioral changes. No issues to flag.

Since `parseEnvString()` already parses this into an unordered dictionary, the comparison should be semantic: parse both strings and compare the resulting key-value pairs, or canonicalize the string to a stable format before storing in `currentSettings`.

Update the equality check in claude-session.ts to call parseEnvString(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) and parseEnvString(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars) (or produce a deterministic serialized form) and compare keys and values so only real changes to environment variables trigger session regeneration, and ensure currentSettings stores the canonicalized form if you choose that route. ```

content.text:
You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers"

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

Your job: Scan for obvious bugs in the diff. Focus ONLY on the diff itself without reading extra context files. Flag only significant bugs; ignore nitpicks and likely false positives. Do not flag issues that you cannot validate without looking at context outside of the git diff.

CRITICAL: We only want HIGH SIGNAL issues. Objective bugs that will cause incorrect behavior at runtime. If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust.

Do NOT post any comments. Just return a list of issues found (or empty list if none).

Here is the diff:

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-handler.ts
- `session!.currentMaxThinkingTokens` → `session.currentMaxThinkingTokens` (removed unnecessary non-null assertion since `session` is already checked in the `if` guard)

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts
- Changed type from `Record<string, unknown>` to `Partial<Options>` with comment
- Moved `hooks: createHooks(sessionId)` from after conditional blocks into the initial object literal
- Changed `sdkOptions.extraArgs = { chrome: null }` to `(sdkOptions as any).extraArgs = { chrome: null }`
- Changed return from `return sdkOptions as unknown as Options` to `return sdkOptions as Options`

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts
- Removed `normalizeValue` function and simplified comparison: `normalizeValue(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) !== normalizeValue(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars)` → `oldSettings.claudeEnvVars !== newSettings?.claudeEnvVars`
- Removed `clearAllSessions()` test helper

## sidecar/agents/claude/message-processor.ts
- In result/success handling: removed `updateSessionStatus(opts.sessionId, "idle")` call. Added comment that status transition to "idle" is owned by `executeOutcome()` in `stream-context.ts`.
- The `ctx.querySucceeded = true` is still set.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-handler.ts
- In `handleCancel`: removed `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` after `abortCodexSession(sessionId)`. Added comment that processQuery's post-loop/catch path owns the status transition via `persistCancellation`.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
- Removed `MODEL_MAPPINGS` lookup table. `resolveCodexModel` now just returns `model || DEFAULT_MODEL` (passthrough).
- The old code was `MODEL_MAPPINGS[model] ?? model` which already fell through to the input for unknown models, so the behavior is identical.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-session.ts
- Removed `clearAllCodexSessions()` test helper

## sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts
- Changed `...(process.env as Record<string, string>)` to use `Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(process.env).filter(...))` to filter out undefined values with a type guard.

## sidecar/db/index.ts
- Removed `export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` re-export

## sidecar/db/session-writer.ts
- **Major change**: Removed application-level SQLITE_BUSY retry loop (spin-wait 200ms, retry once). Now relies on `busy_timeout` PRAGMA (5000ms) at the driver level.
- Removed `updateContextUsage` function entirely.
- Changed `console.log` to `console.debug` for session state logging at startup.
- Removed `NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED` import (was used by deleted `updateContextUsage`).

## sidecar/frontend-client.ts
- **Major change**: Replaced ~290 lines of individual RPC request methods with one-liner delegates to a new private `rpc<TRes>()` helper method.
- `onQuery` signature changed: now takes `tunnel: RpcConnection` as first parameter instead of using `this.requireTunnel()`.
- The `rpc()` method still uses `this.requireTunnel()` for outgoing requests.

## sidecar/index.ts
- Consolidated duplicate SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers into a single `gracefulShutdown` function.
- Updated `FrontendClient.onQuery(rpcTunnel, ...)` call to pass the tunnel explicitly.

## Tests
- Updated tests to match new `onQuery(mockTunnel, handler)` signature
- Updated session-writer test to expect 1 call instead of 2 (no retry)
- Updated message-processor test to expect no `updateSessionStatus` call on result/success

Return your findings as a JSON array of objects with {description, file, reason}. Return [] if no significant bugs found.

1→// shared/protocol.ts
     2→// Canonical Zod schemas for the JSON-RPC 2.0 request/response protocol
     3→// between the frontend (socketService) and sidecar.
     4→//
     5→// These are RPC contracts (request → response), distinct from the
     6→// notification schemas in session-events.ts (one-way push).
     7→
     8→import { z } from "zod";
     9→
    10→import { AgentTypeSchema } from "./enums";
    11→
    12→// ============================================================================
    13→// RPC Method & Notification Constants
    14→// ============================================================================
    15→
    16→/** Methods the frontend can call on the sidecar (request/response) */
    17→export const SIDECAR_METHODS = {
    18→  QUERY: "query",
    19→  CANCEL: "cancel",
    20→  CLAUDE_AUTH: "claudeAuth",
    21→  WORKSPACE_INIT: "workspaceInit",
    22→  CONTEXT_USAGE: "contextUsage",
    23→} as const;
    24→
    25→/** Notifications the frontend sends to the sidecar (fire-and-forget) */
    26→export const SIDECAR_NOTIFICATIONS = {
    27→  UPDATE_PERMISSION_MODE: "updatePermissionMode",
    28→  RESET_GENERATOR: "resetGenerator",
    29→} as const;
    30→
    31→// ============================================================================
    32→// Shared Field Schemas
    33→// ============================================================================

content.text:
You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers"

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

Your job: Look for problems that exist in the INTRODUCED code. This could be security issues, incorrect logic, etc. Only look for issues that fall within the changed code. 

CRITICAL: We only want HIGH SIGNAL issues. Objective bugs that will cause incorrect behavior at runtime. If you are not certain an issue is real, do not flag it. False positives erode trust.

Do NOT post any comments. Return a list of issues found (or empty list if none).

Key changes in the diff:

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts
- Changed type from `Record<string, unknown>` to `Partial<Options>` 
- Moved `hooks: createHooks(sessionId)` into the initial object literal
- `(sdkOptions as any).extraArgs = { chrome: null }` — new `as any` cast
- Return changed from `sdkOptions as unknown as Options` to `sdkOptions as Options`

## sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts
- Removed normalizeValue: old code `normalizeValue(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) !== normalizeValue(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars)` → new code `oldSettings.claudeEnvVars !== newSettings?.claudeEnvVars`
  The old normalizeValue converted both null and undefined to undefined for comparison. The new code compares directly, meaning null !== undefined would now be true (triggering unnecessary session restarts).

## sidecar/agents/claude/message-processor.ts
- Removed `updateSessionStatus(opts.sessionId, "idle")` from result/success handler. Claims executeOutcome() in stream-context.ts owns this. Need to verify that path actually sets idle.

## sidecar/agents/codex/codex-handler.ts
- Removed `updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle")` from handleCancel. Claims processQuery's post-loop/catch path handles it via persistCancellation.

## sidecar/db/session-writer.ts
- Removed application-level SQLITE_BUSY retry loop. Claims busy_timeout PRAGMA (5000ms) handles it.
- Removed `updateContextUsage` function entirely.

## sidecar/db/index.ts
- Removed `export { DEFAULT_DB_PATH }` re-export

## sidecar/frontend-client.ts
- Replaced all individual async RPC methods with one-liner delegates to private `rpc<TRes>()` helper
- Changed `onQuery` to take explicit `tunnel` parameter
- Old methods were `async requestFoo(request: FooRequest): Promise<FooResponse>` 
- New methods are `requestFoo(r: FooRequest) { return this.rpc<FooResponse>(...) }` — note the `async` keyword was removed. The `rpc()` method is async, so these still return promises, but there's a subtle difference: if `this.requireTunnel()` throws synchronously, the old code would throw inside an async function (rejected promise), the new code throws a synchronous exception since the method is no longer async.

## sidecar/index.ts
- Signal handlers changed from `process.on("SIGINT", async () => {...})` to `process.on("SIGINT", () => gracefulShutdown("SIGINT"))` — the inner function is async but the callback wrapper is not. This could mean unhandled promise rejections from gracefulShutdown aren't caught.

Return your findings as a JSON array of objects with {description, file, line_context, reason}. Return [] if no significant bugs found.

1→// sidecar/agents/query-lifecycle.ts
     2→// Shared query lifecycle side effects: cancellation persistence and error
     3→// reporting. These are the state-transition operations that every agent
     4→// handler needs at the edges of its streaming loop (cancel, error, cleanup).
     5→//
     6→// Extracted from duplicate code across claude-handler.ts (2 copies) and
     7→// codex-handler.ts (2 copies). Each handler calls these at the right point
     8→// in its own control flow — no base class, no template method.
     9→
    10→import { FrontendClient } from "../frontend-client";
    11→import { saveAssistantMessage, updateSessionStatus } from "../db/session-writer";
    12→import type { ClassifiedError } from "./error-classifier";
    13→import type { AgentType } from "../protocol";
    14→
    15→// ============================================================================
    16→// Cancellation Persistence
    17→// ============================================================================
    18→
    19→/**
    20→ * Persists a cancellation event: saves a cancelled assistant message to DB,
    21→ * notifies the frontend, and updates session status to idle.
    22→ *
    23→ * This identical sequence was duplicated 4 times across both handlers:
    24→ * - claude-handler.ts post-loop cancel path
    25→ * - claude-handler.ts catch cancel path
    26→ * - codex-handler.ts post-loop abort path
    27→ * - codex-handler.ts catch abort path
    28→ */
    29→export function persistCancellation(
    30→  sessionId: string,
    31→  agentType: AgentType,
    32→  model: string
    33→): void {
    34→  const writeResult = saveAssistantMessage(
    35→    sessionId,
    36→    {
    37→      role: "assistant",
    38→      content: [{ type: "text", text: "" }],
    39→      stop_reason: "cancelled",
    40→    },
    41→    model
    42→  );
    43→
    44→  if (!writeResult.ok) {
    45→    console.error(
    46→      `[persistCancellation] DB write failed for cancelled message: ${writeResult.error}`
    47→    );
    48→  }
    49→
    50→  FrontendClient.sendMessage({
    51→    id: sessionId,
    52→    type: "message",
    53→    agentType,
    54→    data: { type: "cancelled" },
    55→  });
    56→
    57→  const statusResult = updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle");
    58→
    59→  if (!statusResult.ok) {
    60→    FrontendClient.sendError({
    61→      id: sessionId,
    62→      type: "error",
    63→      error: `Session status update failed: ${statusResult.error}`,
    64→      agentType,
    65→      category: "db_write",
    66→    });
    67→  }
    68→}
    69→
    70→// ============================================================================
    71→// Error Reporting
    72→// ============================================================================
    73→
    74→/**
    75→ * Reports a classified error to the frontend and updates session status.
    76→ *
    77→ * The optional `enrichMessage` callback allows handler-specific enrichment
    78→ * without modifying this shared function (Open/Closed). Claude uses it
    79→ * to append process_exit diagnostics (resume state, message count, etc.).
    80→ * Codex passes nothing and gets the classified message as-is.
    81→ */
    82→export function notifyAndRecordError(
    83→  sessionId: string,
    84→  agentType: AgentType,
    85→  classified: ClassifiedError,
    86→  enrichMessage?: (classified: ClassifiedError) => string
    87→): void {
    88→  const errorMessage = enrichMessage
    89→    ? enrichMessage(classified)
    90→    : classified.message;
    91→
    92→  FrontendClient.sendError({
    93→    id: sessionId,
    94→    type: "error",
    95→    error: errorMessage,
    96→    agentType,
    97→    category: classified.category,
    98→  });
    99→
   100→  const statusResult = updateSessionStatus(
   101→    sessionId,
   102→    "error",
   103→    errorMessage,
   104→    classified.category
   105→  );
   106→
   107→  // If the status update itself fails, the session is stuck — notify frontend
   108→  if (!statusResult.ok) {
   109→    FrontendClient.sendError({
   110→      id: sessionId,
   111→      type: "error",
   112→      error: `Session status update failed: ${statusResult.error}`,
   113→      agentType,
   114→      category: "db_write",
   115→    });
   116→  }
   117→}
   118→

     1→# WHAT ARE WE BUILDING?
     2→

     5→This product is for semi-technical people who want to get the job done. They care more about the job output than the technology and code underneath.
     6→
     7→We treat AI chat as a first-class citizen here, code it secondary.
     8→
     9→# TechStack
    10→
    11→Desktop app built with Tauri (Rust) + React frontend + Node.js backend.
    12→
    13→**Package manager: Bun.** Always use `bun` for installing dependencies (`bun add`, `bun install`), running scripts (`bun run`), and executing tools (`bunx`). Never use `npm` or `yarn` — CI runs `bun install --frozen-lockfile` and will fail if `bun.lock` is out of sync.
    14→
    15→**Desktop-only:** We only target the Tauri desktop app. Do not write web-specific fallbacks, browser-mode polling, or `isTauriEnv` conditionals for feature parity. The HTTP/Node.js backend exists as a service layer — not as a standalone web app. Tauri IPC and events are the primary data transport.
    16→
    17→### ts-pattern (Pattern Matching)
    18→
    19→Use `ts-pattern` for switch/case and if/else chains on discriminated unions. Prefer `.exhaustive()` to catch missing cases at compile time.
    20→
    21→```tsx
    22→import { match, P } from "ts-pattern";
    23→
    24→// Discriminated union dispatch
    25→return match(block)
    26→  .with({ type: "text" }, (b) => <TextBlock block={b} />)
    27→  .with({ type: "tool_use" }, (b) => <ToolUseBlock block={b} />)
    28→  .with({ type: "thinking" }, (b) => <ThinkingBlock block={b} />)
    29→  .otherwise(() => null);
    30→
    31→// .exhaustive() — all cases MUST be handled (no default/fallback)
    32→// .otherwise() — intentional fallback for open-ended matching
    33→// P.union("a", "b") — match multiple values
    34→// P.when(pred) — guard conditions
    35→```
    36→
...
</persisted-output>

+
+/**
+ * Resolves a model name for the Codex SDK.
+ * Returns the input unchanged if provided, or the default ("o4-mini").
+ * Unlike Claude (which needs Bedrock/Vertex remapping), Codex model

+export function resolveCodexModel(model: string | undefined): string {

diff --git a/sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts b/sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts
new file mode 100644
index 000000000..e7190433c
--- /dev/null
+++ b/sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,66 @@
+// sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts
+// Factory for the notebook MCP server stdio config.
+//
+// Returns an McpStdioServerConfig that the Claude SDK will spawn as a
+// child process. The notebook server persists a .ipynb file at
+// {workspacePath}/.context/notebook.ipynb so the frontend can read it.
+
+import * as path from "path";
+import * as fs from "fs";
+
+/**
+ * Resolves the absolute path to the bundled notebook MCP server.
+ *
+ * Strategy:
+ * - The sidecar runs from `src-tauri/resources/bin/index.bundled.cjs` (both dev and prod).
+ * - The notebook server is bundled to `notebook-server.bundled.cjs` in the same directory.
+ * - We resolve relative to `process.argv[1]` (the sidecar entry point).
+ */
+function resolveNotebookServerPath(): string | null {
+  // Primary: bundled CJS in the same directory as the sidecar
+  const sidecarDir = path.dirname(process.argv[1]);
+  const bundledPath = path.join(sidecarDir, "notebook-server.bundled.cjs");
+  if (fs.existsSync(bundledPath)) {
+    return bundledPath;
+  }
+
+  console.warn(
+    `[notebook-server] Bundled server not found at ${bundledPath}. ` +
+      `Run 'bun run build:sidecar' to build it.`
+  );
+  return null;
+}
+
+/**
+ * Creates the notebook MCP server config for injection into sdkOptions.mcpServers.
+ * Returns an empty object if the server bundle is not found (graceful degradation).
+ */
+export function createNotebookMCPServer(
+  workingDirectory: string | undefined
+): Record<string, { command: string; args: string[]; env: Record<string, string> }> {
+  if (!workingDirectory) return {};
+
+  const serverPath = resolveNotebookServerPath();
+  if (!serverPath) return {};
+
+  const notebookPath = path.join(workingDirectory, ".context", "notebook.ipynb");
+
+  return {
+    notebook: {
+      command: "node",
+      args: [serverPath],
+      env: {
+        // Spread process.env so the spawned server inherits PATH, HOME,
+        // NODE_PATH, and any other env vars the sidecar was launched with.
+        // The MCP SDK's StdioClientTransport only auto-inherits a small
+        // whitelist (HOME, PATH, SHELL, TERM, USER, LOGNAME on macOS).
+        // Filter out undefined values (process.env values are string | undefined).
+        ...Object.fromEntries(
+          Object.entries(process.env).filter((entry): entry is [string, string] => entry[1] !== undefined)
+        ),
+        NOTEBOOK_CWD: workingDirectory,
+        NOTEBOOK_PATH: notebookPath,
+      },
+    },
+  };
+}

You are reviewing PR #179: "refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers" for CLAUDE.md compliance.

PR description: "Streamlines the sidecar message processing pipeline by removing redundant patterns, consolidating repeated logic across Claude and Codex handlers, and simplifying the frontend notification layer. Overall complexity reduced by 371 lines of code while maintaining full functionality. This is a pure refactoring with no behavioral changes."

All changes are in the `sidecar/` directory. 

Read the CLAUDE.md at `/Users/zvada/Developer/agent/box-ide/.conductor/davao-v4/CLAUDE.md`.

Focus specifically on these CLAUDE.md sections as they apply to sidecar code:
- Database rules (SQLITE_BUSY handling, indexes, denormalization, busy_timeout)
- Sidecar Structure section
- System Architecture / Message Flow
- Performance Guidelines (especially database rules)
- Testing section
- Documentation Policy
- Any rules about package manager (bun vs npm)

Search the codebase for usages of these removed exports to verify they aren't needed.

content.content:
1→// sidecar/agents/query-lifecycle.ts
     2→// Shared query lifecycle side effects: cancellation persistence and error
     3→// reporting. These are the state-transition operations that every agent
     4→// handler needs at the edges of its streaming loop (cancel, error, cleanup).
     5→//
     6→// Extracted from duplicate code across claude-handler.ts (2 copies) and
     7→// codex-handler.ts (2 copies). Each handler calls these at the right point
     8→// in its own control flow — no base class, no template method.
     9→
    10→import { FrontendClient } from "../frontend-client";
    11→import { saveAssistantMessage, updateSessionStatus } from "../db/session-writer";
    12→import type { ClassifiedError } from "./error-classifier";
    13→import type { AgentType } from "../protocol";
    14→
    15→// ============================================================================
    16→// Cancellation Persistence
    17→// ============================================================================
    18→
    19→/**
    20→ * Persists a cancellation event: saves a cancelled assistant message to DB,
    21→ * notifies the frontend, and updates session status to idle.
    22→ *
    23→ * This identical sequence was duplicated 4 times across both handlers:
    24→ * - claude-handler.ts post-loop cancel path
    25→ * - claude-handler.ts catch cancel path
    26→ * - codex-handler.ts post-loop abort path
    27→ * - codex-handler.ts catch abort path
    28→ */
    29→export function persistCancellation(
    30→  sessionId: string,
    31→  agentType: AgentType,
    32→  model: string
    33→): void {
    34→  const writeResult = saveAssistantMessage(
    35→    sessionId,
    36→    {
    37→      role: "assistant",
    38→      content: [{ type: "text", text: "" }],
    39→      stop_reason: "cancelled",
    40→    },
    41→    model
    42→  );
    43→
    44→  if (!writeResult.ok) {
    45→    console.error(
    46→      `[persistCancellation] DB write failed for cancelled message: ${writeResult.error}`
    47→    );
    48→  }
    49→
    50→  FrontendClient.sendMessage({
    51→    id: sessionId,
    52→    type: "message",
    53→    agentType,
    54→    data: { type: "cancelled" },
    55→  });
    56→
    57→  const statusResult = updateSessionStatus(sessionId, "idle");
    58→
    59→  if (!statusResult.ok) {
    60→    FrontendClient.sendError({
    61→      id: sessionId,
    62→      type: "error",
    63→      error: `Session status update failed: ${statusResult.error}`,
    64→      agentType,
    65→      category: "db_write",
    66→    });
    67→  }
    68→}
    69→
    70→// ============================================================================
    71→// Error Reporting
    72→// ============================================================================
    73→
    74→/**
    75→ * Reports a classified error to the frontend and updates session status.
    76→ *
    77→ * The optional `enrichMessage` callback allows handler-specific enrichment
    78→ * without modifying this shared function (Open/Closed). Claude uses it
    79→ * to append process_exit diagnostics (resume state, message count, etc.).
    80→ * Codex passes nothing and gets the classified message as-is.
    81→ */
    82→export function notifyAndRecordError(
    83→  sessionId: string,
    84→  agentType: AgentType,
    85→  classified: ClassifiedError,
    86→  enrichMessage?: (classified: ClassifiedError) => string
    87→): void {
    88→  const errorMessage = enrichMessage
    89→    ? enrichMessage(classified)
    90→    : classified.message;
    91→
    92→  FrontendClient.sendError({
    93→    id: sessionId,
    94→    type: "error",
    95→    error: errorMessage,
    96→    agentType,
    97→    category: classified.category,
    98→  });
    99→
   100→  const statusResult = updateSessionStatus(
   101→    sessionId,
   102→    "error",
   103→    errorMessage,
   104→    classified.category
   105→  );
   106→
   107→  // If the status update itself fails, the session is stuck — notify frontend
   108→  if (!statusResult.ok) {
   109→    FrontendClient.sendError({
   110→      id: sessionId,
   111→      type: "error",
   112→      error: `Session status update failed: ${statusResult.error}`,
   113→      agentType,
   114→      category: "db_write",
   115→    });
   116→  }
   117→}
   118→

📝 Walkthrough ## Walkthrough This pull request refactors session management, simplifies RPC communication patterns, removes utility cleanup functions, and optimizes database operations. Key changes include consolidating frontend RPC methods into a generic helper, deferring session status updates to a centralized handler, and removing retry logic from database writes in favor of driver-level timeout handling. ## Changes |Cohort / File(s)|Summary| |---|---| |**Claude Agent Session Management**
`sidecar/agents/claude/claude-handler.ts`, `sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts`, `sidecar/agents/claude/claude-sdk-options.ts`|Removed non-null assertion in handler, simplified settings change detection for claudeEnvVars, changed sdkOptions typing from Record to Partial with hooks initialized inline. Removed exported clearAllSessions utility function.| |**Message Processing & Status Deferral**
`sidecar/agents/claude/message-processor.ts`, `sidecar/test/message-processor.test.ts`|Removed runtime session status update to "idle" from message-processor; status transition now deferred to executeOutcome() in stream-context.ts. Updated test assertions to reflect new ownership of status transitions.| |**Codex Agent Management**
`sidecar/agents/codex/codex-handler.ts`, `sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts`, `sidecar/agents/codex/codex-session.ts`|Removed updateSessionStatus call in cancellation handler, deferring to persistCancellation flow. Removed MODEL_MAPPINGS and simplified resolveCodexModel to direct passthrough with DEFAULT_MODEL constant. Removed exported clearAllCodexSessions utility function.| |**Database & Session Writer Refactoring**
`sidecar/db/index.ts`, `sidecar/db/session-writer.ts`, `sidecar/test/session-writer.test.ts`|Removed export of DEFAULT_DB_PATH. Simplified updateSessionStatus by eliminating SQLITE_BUSY retry loop (relying on driver-level busy_timeout PRAGMA). Removed updateContextUsage function, NOTIFY_SESSION_UPDATED import, in-flight frontend notifications, and verbose instrumentation. Updated tests to expect single DB attempt instead of retries.| |**Frontend RPC Communication**
`sidecar/frontend-client.ts`, `sidecar/index.ts`, `sidecar/test/frontend-client.test.ts`|Consolidated repetitive RPC method implementations into a single generic rpc helper. Updated onQuery signature to accept tunnel parameter as first argument. All requestX methods now delegate to rpc helper with method enum, input, and timeout. Updated test invocations to pass tunnel parameter.| |**Infrastructure & Environment**
`sidecar/agents/notebook-server.ts`, `sidecar/agents/opendevs-tools/simulator.ts`|Added filtering of undefined environment values before spreading into notebook server env. Removed type literal assertions (as const) from simulator tool content type declarations, simplifying from "text" as const to plain "text".| ## Estimated code review effort 🎯 3 (Moderate) | ⏱️ ~25 minutes ## Poem > 🐰 *Whiskers twitching with delight,* > *RPC helpers consolidated tight,* > *Sessions defer their status calls,* > *Cleanup functions leave the halls,* > *Database writes, now lean and quick—* > *A refactor done with rabbit's trick!* 🎀

---

## INVESTIGATION REPORT: `claudeEnvVars` Comparison Issue

I've traced the complete data flow of `claudeEnvVars` through the system. Here are the key findings:

### Data Flow Summary

```
Frontend (no claudeEnvVars) 
  → Sidecar receives options.claudeEnvVars (raw string from protocol)
  → Stored in session.currentSettings.claudeEnvVars
  → Compared in settingsChanged() via raw string equality
  → Parsed by parseEnvString() only when building the actual environment
```

### Detailed Flow Analysis

**1. Origin of `claudeEnvVars`**

`claudeEnvVars` is defined in:
- `/shared/protocol.ts` (line 57, 121): Optional string in `QueryOptionsSchema` and `WorkspaceInitRequestSchema`
- The **frontend does not provide claudeEnvVars** - I found zero references to it in `src/` code
- It would only arrive if the frontend explicitly sends it in the query options, which currently doesn't happen

**2. How it enters the sidecar**

In `/sidecar/index.ts` (line 213):
```typescript
claudeEnvVars: request.options.claudeEnvVars,  // Passes raw string through
```

The raw string comes directly from the JSON-RPC request.

**3. Storage in Session State**

In `/sidecar/agents/claude/claude-handler.ts` (lines 199-204):
```typescript
const newSession: SessionState = {
  currentSettings: {
    claudeEnvVars: options.claudeEnvVars,  // Stored as-is (raw string)
    additionalDirectories: options.additionalDirectories,
    chromeEnabled: options.chromeEnabled,
    strictDataPrivacy: options.strictDataPrivacy,
  },
  // ...
};
```

**4. The Comparison in `settingsChanged()`**

In `/sidecar/agents/claude/claude-session.ts` (lines 109-111):
```typescript
if (oldSettings.claudeEnvVars !== newSettings?.claudeEnvVars) {
  return true;
}
```

This is a **raw string comparison** using `!==`.

**5. Parsing happens ONLY for environment construction**

In `/sidecar/agents/env-builder.ts` (lines 107-113):
```typescript
if (options?.claudeEnvVars) {
  const parsed = parseEnvString(options.claudeEnvVars);
  for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(parsed)) {
    if (value === "") delete env[key];
    else env[key] = value;
  }
}
```

The parsed object is used **immediately to build the environment object** - it's never serialized back to a string or stored.

---

### CodeRabbit's Concern: Is It a Real Problem?

**THE CONCERN:** If the same logical env vars are serialized in different orders (e.g., "A=1\nB=2" vs "B=2\nA=1"), the raw string comparison would incorrectly detect settings as changed, causing unnecessary generator regeneration.

**THE VERDICT: This concern is THEORETICAL, not practical.**

#### Why it can't happen in practice:

1. **Frontend doesn't send `claudeEnvVars` at all** - I found zero frontend code that constructs this field. The query is sent via `socketService.sendQuery()` with only `{ cwd, model }` options (line 255 of `/src/features/session/api/session.queries.ts`).

2. **No intermediate re-serialization** - Once `claudeEnvVars` enters as a raw string:
   - It's stored in `session.currentSettings.claudeEnvVars` (never modified)
   - It's only parsed via `parseEnvString()` to build the actual environment
   - The parsed object is never converted back to a string
   - Therefore, the string stored in `currentSettings` remains exactly as received

3. **Only comparison happens at query time** - On the second and subsequent queries:
   - `newSettings?.claudeEnvVars` comes from `options.claudeEnvVars` in the incoming request
   - It's compared to `oldSettings.claudeEnvVars` from the previous query
   - Both are raw strings that came directly from the protocol

4. **If order could change, it would be at the protocol boundary** - The only way different orders could exist is if:
   - The frontend code changed to send `claudeEnvVars` AND
   - Different parts of the code serialized it in different orders AND
   - The sidecar received these differently-ordered strings
   - This doesn't happen currently because the frontend never sends it

#### Realistic Scenarios

The string would only differ if:
- **User edits settings between queries** - They change their environment variables in the settings UI, and the frontend sends the new string. But the order would be determined by however the UI serializes them (probably in the same order every time).
- **Different request comes from an external client** - If an external tool or API client sends queries, they might serialize env vars differently. But:
  - That tool would have to explicitly set `claudeEnvVars` in the query options
  - It would need to send it in a different order than previous requests
  - This would be intentional or a bug in that external client, not this codebase

---

### Impact Assessment

**If it did happen**, the impact would be:

1. **Generator would be unnecessarily regenerated** (claude-handler.ts, line 119, 127):
   - The session would be terminated (line 195)
   - A new generator would be created (line 210-212)
   - Cost: ~100-200ms generator recreation + Claude SDK initialization
   - No data loss, but causes UI spinner and context continuity break

2. **Not catastrophic** - The system is designed to handle generator resets:
   - `shouldResetGenerator` flag is already a user-triggered mechanism (line 187)
   - Session recovery via auto-resume handles this gracefully

---

### Recommendation

**This is a low-risk issue:**

**Option 1 (Do Nothing - Recommended):**
- The frontend doesn't send `claudeEnvVars` currently
- No intermediate serialization exists
- The problem can't manifest without significant changes to the protocol usage
- Cost of fix vs. risk is unfavorable

**Option 2 (Defensive Programming):**
- Parse both old and new strings into objects, then compare:
  ```typescript
  const oldEnvVars = oldSettings.claudeEnvVars ? parseEnvString(oldSettings.claudeEnvVars) : {};
  const newEnvVars = newSettings?.claudeEnvVars ? parseEnvString(newSettings?.claudeEnvVars) : {};
  
  // Deep equality check (compare as objects, not strings)
  if (JSON.stringify(oldEnvVars) !== JSON.stringify(newEnvVars)) {
    return true;
  }
  ```
- **Pros:** Handles ordering invariance, semantic comparison
- **Cons:** Extra parsing overhead on every query, object key ordering in JSON.stringify could theoretically differ (use sorted keys if fixing)
- Cost: ~1-2ms per comparison, minimal improvement in correctness

**Option 3 (Normalized Serialization):**
- If claudeEnvVars needs to come from the frontend in the future, normalize it before storage:
  ```typescript
  const normalizeCflaude = (s: string): string => {
    return Object.entries(parseEnvString(s))
      .sort(([a], [b]) => a.localeCompare(b))
      .map(([k, v]) => `${k}=${v}`)
      .join('\n');
  };
  ```
- Then compare normalized strings
- Cost: Parsing overhead, but only at session creation (not every query)

---

### Current Status

The code works correctly today because:
1. The frontend doesn't send `claudeEnvVars` - only the sidecar-specific RPC methods (`workspaceInit`, `claudeAuth`) can receive it
2. No re-serialization happens after the raw string arrives
3. The same string instance is compared across queries

The raw string comparison is fine for the **current usage pattern**, but if the protocol evolves to have the frontend send environment overrides, then Option 2 or 3 would be worth implementing.

content.text:
Both investigations are complete. Here's my assessment:

---

### Comment #2: `claudeEnvVars` raw string comparison — **Not actionable**

CodeRabbit's concern: if env vars arrive in different key orders (`"A=1\nB=2"` vs `"B=2\nA=1"`), the raw string comparison triggers unnecessary session regeneration.

**Why it's not a real problem:**

1. **The frontend never sends `claudeEnvVars`** — zero references in `src/`. The field exists in the protocol schema but is unused from the UI.
2. **No intermediate re-serialization** — the raw string from the JSON-RPC request is stored as-is in `currentSettings` and compared directly against the next request's raw string. Same source → same serialization → same string.
3. **The old code was also a string comparison** — the removed `normalizeValue()` only handled `null`/`undefined` coercion, not key-order normalization. So this "issue" existed before the PR too (pre-existing, not introduced).
4. **If it were ever to matter**, the fix belongs at the protocol boundary (normalize on ingestion), not in `settingsChanged()`.

This is a theoretical concern about code that isn't exercised. Fixing it adds parsing overhead for zero benefit.

---

### Comment #3: `||` vs `??` in `resolveCodexModel` — **Actionable (minor)**

CodeRabbit's concern: `model || DEFAULT_MODEL` treats empty string `""` as falsy.

**Investigation findings:**

- The Zod schema already prevents empty strings: `z.string().min(1).optional()` — so `""` can never reach this function in practice
- The old code also treated `""` as falsy (`if (!model) return DEFAULT_MODEL`)
- Claude's equivalent `mapModelForProvider` also uses `if (!simpleModel)` (same behavior)

**However**, using `??` is the right call:
- It's more precise — clearly says "only fall back on `undefined`/`null`"
- It's idiomatic modern TypeScript best practice for optional parameters
- Tiny change, zero risk, better communicates intent

Let me fix it.

Investigate the `resolveCodexModel` function in `sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts`. CodeRabbit flagged that `model || DEFAULT_MODEL` should be `model ?? DEFAULT_MODEL` because `||` treats empty string as falsy.

I need to understand:
1. Read the current `codex-models.ts` file
2. Where is `resolveCodexModel` called? What values can `model` have?
3. Trace the `model` parameter back to its source - where does it originate? Check the protocol schema, frontend code, backend code.
4. Can `model` ever be an empty string `""`? If so, what should happen - should empty string fall back to default, or should it be passed through?
5. What does the Codex/OpenAI SDK do if it receives an empty string as a model name? Would it error?
6. Read the old code (before the PR) - what did the old `MODEL_MAPPINGS` approach do with empty string?
7. Check the Claude equivalent - how does `resolveClaudeModel` handle empty/undefined?

1→// sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
     2→// Model name resolution for OpenAI Codex agent.
     3→
     4→const DEFAULT_MODEL = "o4-mini";
     5→
     6→/**
     7→ * Resolves a model name for the Codex SDK.
     8→ * Returns the input unchanged if provided, or the default ("o4-mini").
     9→ * Unlike Claude (which needs Bedrock/Vertex remapping), Codex model
    10→ * names are passed through directly to the SDK.
    11→ */
    12→export function resolveCodexModel(model: string | undefined): string {
    13→  return model || DEFAULT_MODEL;
    14→}

1→// shared/protocol.ts
     2→// Canonical Zod schemas for the JSON-RPC 2.0 request/response protocol
     3→// between the frontend (socketService) and sidecar.
     4→//
     5→// These are RPC contracts (request → response), distinct from the
     6→// notification schemas in session-events.ts (one-way push).
     7→
     8→import { z } from "zod";
     9→
    10→import { AgentTypeSchema } from "./enums";
    11→
    12→// ============================================================================
    13→// RPC Method & Notification Constants
    14→// ============================================================================
    15→
    16→/** Methods the frontend can call on the sidecar (request/response) */
    17→export const SIDECAR_METHODS = {
    18→  QUERY: "query",
    19→  CANCEL: "cancel",
    20→  CLAUDE_AUTH: "claudeAuth",
    21→  WORKSPACE_INIT: "workspaceInit",
    22→  CONTEXT_USAGE: "contextUsage",
    23→} as const;
    24→
    25→/** Notifications the frontend sends to the sidecar (fire-and-forget) */
    26→export const SIDECAR_NOTIFICATIONS = {
    27→  UPDATE_PERMISSION_MODE: "updatePermissionMode",
    28→  RESET_GENERATOR: "resetGenerator",
    29→} as const;
    30→
    31→// ============================================================================
    32→// Shared Field Schemas
    33→// ============================================================================
    34→
    35→/** Matches the Claude Agent SDK's PermissionMode union type. */
    36→export const PermissionModeSchema = z.enum([
    37→  "default",
    38→  "acceptEdits",
    39→  "bypassPermissions",
    40→  "plan",
    41→  "dontAsk",
    42→]);
    43→export type PermissionMode = z.infer<typeof PermissionModeSchema>;
    44→
    45→// ============================================================================
    46→// Query (frontend → sidecar)
    47→// ============================================================================
    48→
    49→/** Options passed alongside a query request. */
    50→export const QueryOptionsSchema = z.object({
    51→  cwd: z.string().min(1),
    52→  model: z.string().min(1).optional(),
    53→  maxThinkingTokens: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
    54→  maxTurns: z.number().int().positive().optional(),
    55→  turnId: z.string().min(1).optional(),
    56→  permissionMode: PermissionModeSchema.optional(),
    57→  claudeEnvVars: z.string().optional(),
    58→  ghToken: z.string().optional(),
    59→  opendevsEnv: z.record(z.string(), z.string()).optional(),
    60→  additionalDirectories: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
    61→  chromeEnabled: z.boolean().optional(),
    62→  strictDataPrivacy: z.boolean().optional(),
    63→  shouldResetGenerator: z.boolean().optional(),
    64→  resume: z.string().min(1).optional(),
    65→  resumeSessionAt: z.string().min(1).optional(),
    66→});
    67→export type QueryOptions = z.infer<typeof QueryOptionsSchema>;
    68→
    69→export const QueryRequestSchema = z.object({
    70→  type: z.literal("query"),
    71→  id: z.string().min(1),
    72→  agentType: AgentTypeSchema,
    73→  prompt: z.string().min(1),
    74→  options: QueryOptionsSchema,
    75→});
    76→export type QueryRequest = z.infer<typeof QueryRequestSchema>;
    77→
    78→/** Synchronous ACK/reject response for query method. */
    79→export const QueryAckResponseSchema = z.object({
    80→  accepted: z.boolean(),
    81→  reason: z.string().optional(),
    82→});
    83→export type QueryAckResponse = z.infer<typeof QueryAckResponseSchema>;
    84→
    85→// ============================================================================
    86→// Cancel (frontend → sidecar)
    87→// ============================================================================
    88→
    89→export const CancelRequestSchema = z.object({
    90→  type: z.literal("cancel"),
    91→  id: z.string().min(1),
    92→  agentType: AgentTypeSchema,
    93→});
    94→export type CancelRequest = z.infer<typeof CancelRequestSchema>;
    95→
    96→// ============================================================================
    97→// Claude Auth (frontend → sidecar)
    98→// ============================================================================
    99→

content.content:
refactor(sidecar): streamline message processing and agent handlers

Simplifies the sidecar message flow by consolidating repeated patterns across
Claude and Codex handlers, removing intermediate layers, and reducing overall
complexity. Refactors frontend-client notifications and session-writer to use
direct message formatting without verbose wrapper classes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Haiku 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

input.description: Show the exact diff of what changed

content.content:
diff --git a/sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts b/sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
index 4d0495e62..3c6120bc7 100644
--- a/sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
+++ b/sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
@@ -1,29 +1,14 @@
 // sidecar/agents/codex/codex-models.ts
-// Model name mapping for OpenAI Codex agent.
-
-/**
- * Maps simple model aliases to full OpenAI model identifiers.
- * The frontend sends short names (e.g., "o4-mini"); this maps them
- * to the exact model string the Codex SDK expects.
- *
- * Unlike Claude which needs Bedrock/Vertex mapping, Codex models
- * are used directly — this mostly serves as a validation/default layer.
- */
-const MODEL_MAPPINGS: Record<string, string> = {
-  "o4-mini": "o4-mini",
-  o3: "o3",
-  "gpt-4.1": "gpt-4.1",
-  "gpt-4.1-mini": "gpt-4.1-mini",
-  "gpt-4.1-nano": "gpt-4.1-nano",
-};
+// Model name resolution for OpenAI Codex agent.
 
 const DEFAULT_MODEL = "o4-mini";
 
 /**
  * Resolves a model name for the Codex SDK.
- * Returns the input if it's already a valid model, or the default.
+ * Returns the input unchanged if provided, or the default ("o4-mini").
+ * Unlike Claude (which needs Bedrock/Vertex remapping), Codex model
+ * names are passed through directly to the SDK.
  */
 export function resolveCodexModel(model: string | undefined): string {
-  if (!model) return DEFAULT_MODEL;
-  return MODEL_MAPPINGS[model] ?? model;
+  return model || DEFAULT_MODEL;
 }

input.prompt: Search the web for the correct model identifier/name for OpenAI's GPT 5.3 model. I need the exact string that the OpenAI API expects (e.g., "gpt-5.3", "gpt-5.3-mini", etc.). Check OpenAI's documentation or recent announcements. Also check if there are variants like "gpt-5.3-mini" or "gpt-5.3-nano". Return the exact model ID string(s).

content.text:
Here are the findings on OpenAI's GPT-5.3 model identifiers:

## Exact API Model ID Strings

There are **two** GPT-5.3 model IDs available in the OpenAI API:

1. **`gpt-5.3-chat-latest`** -- This is the GPT-5.3 "Instant" model, the same snapshot currently powering ChatGPT. It launched on March 3, 2026. OpenAI has a dedicated docs page for it. Note: OpenAI recommends `gpt-5.2` for general API usage but makes `gpt-5.3-chat-latest` available to test the latest chat improvements.

2. **`gpt-5.3-codex`** -- This is the specialized agentic coding model (GPT-5.3-Codex). It is only available via the Responses API (not Chat Completions). It supports `low`, `medium`, `high`, and `xhigh` reasoning effort settings, plus function calling, structured outputs, streaming, and prompt caching.

## No GPT-5.3-mini or GPT-5.3-nano Variants

There are **no** `gpt-5.3-mini` or `gpt-5.3-nano` variants. The mini and nano model IDs exist at the base GPT-5 level only:
- `gpt-5-mini`
- `gpt-5-nano`

These are separate, smaller models in the GPT-5 family -- not point-release variants of 5.3.

## Summary

| Model ID | Description |
|---|---|
| `gpt-5.3-chat-latest` | GPT-5.3 Instant (ChatGPT snapshot) |
| `gpt-5.3-codex` | Agentic coding model (Responses API only) |

Sources:
- [GPT-5.3 Chat Model | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.3-chat-latest)
- [GPT-5.3-Codex Model | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.3-codex)
- [API model: gpt-5.3-chat-latest available - OpenAI Developer Community](https://community.openai.com/t/api-model-gpt-5-3-chat-latest-available-aka-instant-on-chatgpt/1375606)
- [Introducing GPT-5.3-Codex | OpenAI](https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-3-codex/)
- [Models | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models)
- [OpenAI GPT-5 Model Guide (2026) | NxCode](https://www.nxcode.io/resources/news/openai-gpt-5-model-guide-which-to-use-2026)

input.prompt: Search the web for the correct model identifier/name for OpenAI's latest GPT 5.4 model. I need the exact API string that the OpenAI API expects. Check if there's a "gpt-5.4-codex" or similar coding-specific variant. Look at OpenAI's model documentation, API reference, and any recent announcements about GPT 5.4. Return the exact model ID string(s) available.

content.text:
Here are the findings on OpenAI's GPT-5.4 model identifiers.

## GPT-5.4 API Model Strings

There are **two primary GPT-5.4 API identifiers**:

| Model | API String | Snapshot Version |
|---|---|---|
| **GPT-5.4** | `gpt-5.4` | `gpt-5.4-2026-03-05` |
| **GPT-5.4 Pro** | `gpt-5.4-pro` | `gpt-5.4-pro-2026-03-05` |

The date suffix `2026-03-05` refers to the release date (March 5, 2026). You can use either the snapshot version with the date or the alias without the date to get the latest version.

## No "gpt-5.4-codex" Variant Exists

There is **no** `gpt-5.4-codex` model identifier. OpenAI changed their approach with 5.4: instead of releasing a separate coding-specific variant, GPT-5.4 itself subsumes the coding capabilities of the previous `gpt-5.3-codex` model. According to OpenAI's documentation, GPT-5.4 is "the first mainline reasoning model that incorporates the frontier coding capabilities of GPT-5.3-codex" and is now the default model powering Codex and Codex CLI.

## Older Codex-Specific Models (Still Available)

For reference, the previous coding-specific model variants that still exist in the API are:

- `gpt-5.3-codex` -- the previous flagship coding model (Responses API only)
- `gpt-5.2-codex`
- `gpt-5.1-codex`
- `gpt-5-codex`
- `gpt-5-codex-mini` -- smaller/cheaper variant

## Summary

For GPT-5.4, use **`gpt-5.4`** for both general-purpose and coding tasks. Use **`gpt-5.4-pro`** for maximum performance on the most complex tasks. There is no separate `-codex` variant -- GPT-5.4 natively includes state-of-the-art coding capabilities.

Sources:
- [GPT-5.4 Model | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4)
- [GPT-5.4 Pro Model | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4-pro)
- [Using GPT-5.4 | OpenAI API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/latest-model/)
- [Introducing GPT-5.4 | OpenAI](https://openai.com/index/introducing-gpt-5-4/)
- [Codex Models](https://developers.openai.com/codex/models/)
